pattern_recognition: This problem falls under the category of 'Two Pointers' and 'Hashing'. It signals the need to find pairs in an array that meet a specific condition (sum to a target). The presence of a unique solution indicates that we can leverage data structures for efficient lookups.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check every possible pair of numbers to see if they sum to the target.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through each element and for each element, iterate through the rest of the elements to find a pair that sums to the target.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use a hash map to store the numbers and their indices, allowing for O(1) average time complexity lookups.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through the array, for each number, calculate the complement (target - number) and check if it exists in the hash map. If it does, return the indices.

dry_run: For input nums = [2, 7, 11, 15] and target = 9: Start with an empty hash map. Iterate through nums: 2 is added to the map. Next, 7 is checked; its complement (2) is found in the map. Return indices [0, 1].
edge_cases: Consider scenarios like an empty input array, an array with a single element, duplicates that can form valid pairs, very large input sizes (up to 10^4), and potential overflow issues with large integers.
common_pitfalls: Common mistakes include: 1) Not checking for the same element being used twice, which is not allowed. 2) Failing to handle negative numbers correctly. 3) Overlooking the need for a hash map for efficient lookups, leading to a brute force solution that is too slow.
